description:
You are the newly-appointed backup administrator of an on-premises system that stores its TLS assets in users’ home folders.  
Your first duty is to create a **version-stamped, cryptographically-auditable archive** of a miniature private-PKI that you will generate on the fly.  
Everything must be reproducible from a clean machine that only has the standard OpenSSL and GNU core utilities installed.

TARGET DATE STAMP  
For every file name that contains a date, **hard-code the date 20240323** (YYYYMMDD).  
The automated checker looks for that exact string.

WORKING TREE  
1. Create the working area:  
   /home/user/backup-work/            (root of all material you generate)  
   /home/user/backup-work/bundle/     (will contain the public certificate chain)  

2. Generate the following five PKI artefacts **inside /home/user/backup-work** (not inside “bundle”):  
   a. rootCA.key   2048-bit RSA private key  
   b. rootCA.crt   self-signed X.509, CN = BackupRootCA, validity 365 days  
   c. server.key   2048-bit RSA private key  
   d. server.csr   Certificate Signing Request, CN = backup.local  
   e. server.crt   X.509 issued by rootCA, validity 180 days  

3. Copy ONLY the two public certificates into the bundle directory so that:  
   /home/user/backup-work/bundle/rootCA.crt  
   /home/user/backup-work/bundle/server.crt  

ARCHIVE AREA  
4. Make sure the archive target directory exists:  
   /home/user/archives/  

5. Create a gzip-compressed tar archive named  
   /home/user/archives/cert-backup-20240323.tar.gz  
   It must contain **all five PKI artefacts** exactly once and preserve their
   relative paths (the bundle/ sub-directory must appear inside the archive).

MANIFEST (THIS FILE WILL BE AUTO-GRADED)  
6. After the archive is produced, build a manifest file named  
   /home/user/archives/cert-backup-20240323.manifest.csv  

   Every generated artefact listed below must occupy one line in that file.
   The format of **each line is EXACTLY**:

      <relative_path_from_/home/user/backup-work>,<sha256_hex>,<file_size_bytes>

   • Use comma (`,`) as the single delimiter; no spaces.  
   • `<relative_path…>` must match what is stored in the tarball, e.g.  
     `rootCA.key` or `bundle/rootCA.crt`.  
   • `<sha256_hex>` must be the full lower-case 64-character SHA-256 digest.  
   • `<file_size_bytes>` is the exact byte count reported by the `stat --printf="%s"` utility.  
   • Sort the five lines in straightforward lexicographic order by the relative path.  
   • Ensure the file ends with a single trailing newline character.

When you are done, the final directory layout must look like this (⇢ = “contains”):

/home/user/backup-work ⇢  rootCA.key  
                       |  rootCA.crt  
                       |  server.key  
                       |  server.csr  
                       |  server.crt  
                       └─ bundle ⇢  rootCA.crt  
                                   server.crt  

/home/user/archives ⇢   cert-backup-20240323.tar.gz  
                       cert-backup-20240323.manifest.csv  

VERIFICATION HINTS  
• The grading robot will untar /home/user/archives/cert-backup-20240323.tar.gz into a temp folder, calculate fresh SHA-256 digests for each file it finds there, and cross-check them against the values in the manifest.  
• Any deviation in file names, date stamp, column order, delimiter, digest length or missing trailing newline will be treated as a failure.  

Your task is complete when both the tarball and its manifest exist with the exact names shown above and the manifest passes the checker’s integrity test.
truth:
EXPECTED FILES (relative to /home/user/backup-work):
1. rootCA.key
2. rootCA.crt
3. server.key
4. server.csr
5. server.crt
6. bundle/rootCA.crt      (copy)
7. bundle/server.crt      (copy)

ARCHIVE:
/home/user/archives/cert-backup-20240323.tar.gz
  └─ must contain the five primary artefacts (items 1-5) with the paths above.

MANIFEST:
/home/user/archives/cert-backup-20240323.manifest.csv
  ├─ exactly 5 non-blank lines  
  ├─ each line matches regex:
      ^(bundle/)?(rootCA.key|rootCA.crt|server.key|server.csr|server.crt),[0-9a-f]{64},[0-9]+$
  ├─ lines sorted lexicographically (plain `sort` order)  
  └─ file ends with a single trailing newline.

GRADER LOGIC (overview – not shown to agent):
1. Check existence of the two artefacts inside /home/user/archives.  
2. Untar the gzip archive to /tmp/extract.  
3. Ensure extracted file set equals {rootCA.key, rootCA.crt, server.key, server.csr, server.crt}.  
4. Parse manifest; confirm 5 entries & order.  
5. For each line, recompute sha256sum on the counterpart file from /tmp/extract and compare.  
6. Confirm reported byte counts match `stat --printf="%s"` results.  
7. Ensure every path in manifest exists in the tarball, and vice-versa.

No pre-existing files are placed in /home/user; the filesystem is initially empty except for standard skeleton folders.  
Permissions are not graded as long as files are readable by the current user.
name: task_000000_ffa6e96d
